challenge_text: Schedule a --delay message, kill -9 the broker before it fires, restart, and confirm the message still fires after restart (documented at-most-once-modulo-crash). SUCCESS: deferred file survives the kill; scheduler re-arms from deferred/ on restart; the message fires (at most one duplicate only if the crash landed between mv and delivery).

errors_encountered.operation: kill -9 <single top broker pid>; ls deferred/ over 9s
errors_encountered.error: orphaned scheduler survived and silently fired+drained the deferred record while operator believed broker was down
errors_encountered.context: killing only $BPID reparents sched.sh, a 2nd serve proc, and socat to PID 1; the orphan scheduler kept the deadline alive and consumed the deferred file into no connected subscriber

friction_points.description: Overdue-on-restart fire RACES the reconnecting subscriber and is LOST most of the time. True power cut (all procs killed atomically): deferred file survives the down window and the scheduler re-arms on restart (good) — but the overdue record fires on the scheduler's first deferred/ scan, which races the socat-accept + handler + FIFO-registration of a fast-reconnecting subscriber. Measured 2/5 fast-reconnect trials caught the message, 3/5 lost it (a.out/c.out empty, deferred drained). For my boat-sensor use case (sub auto-reconnects on boot) this means the overdue message is silently missed ~60% of the time.
friction_points.workaround: None at the user level. Could be mitigated if the broker delayed firing overdue-on-restart records by a small grace window, or held them until at least one sub is registered, or exposed them via outbox until consumed. Docs should at minimum QUANTIFY that a reconnecting sub almost always loses this race.

friction_points.description: Killing ONLY the top broker pid (the obvious 'kill the broker' and the realistic supervisor-kills-launched-pid case) orphans sched.sh + a second serve proc + the socat acceptor to PID 1. The orphan scheduler keeps running, fires the overdue --delay record into nobody, and DRAINS deferred/ — so a later 'restart' has nothing to re-arm and the message is silently lost, while leaked orphans linger. Observed: after kill -9 <BPID>, ps showed pids 16(sched)/17(serve)/20(socat) reparented to PID 1; deferred file vanished at t=6 with no broker that the operator started.
friction_points.workaround: Operator must kill the whole process group/tree (pkill -f 'src/shellmux serve'; pkill -f 'src/sched.sh'; pkill socat), or start the broker under a setsid/process-group and kill the group. DEMO's 'serve &' / 'kill' snippets do not warn about this.

friction_points.description: No 'subscriber ready' signal (known/punted F5) compounds fp-001 in the crash-recovery path specifically: because overdue records fire instantly on re-arm, the lack of any readiness handshake turns the punted ergonomic gap into actual data loss on the recovery path, not just a hardcoded sleep annoyance.
friction_points.workaround: Hardcode a sleep before pub in normal operation; but on restart the broker itself fires before any sub can signal readiness, so the sleep workaround does not help the overdue case.

wished_for: A small grace window (or hold-until-first-subscriber) for overdue-on-restart records so a reconnecting sub does not lose them ~60% of the time.
DEMO/README to QUANTIFY the overdue-on-restart loss probability for a reconnecting subscriber, not just say 'into whoever is connected then'.
A documented, single safe way to stop the broker (kill the group/tree) since killing the top pid orphans the scheduler and silently drains deferred state.
An outbox/retained slot for overdue-on-restart records, visible via ls, instead of fire-into-void.
what_worked_well: Deferred file genuinely survives a full kill -9 (count stayed 1 across a 9s down window) and the scheduler re-arms instantly on restart — the disk-is-authoritative discipline holds.
Idle CPU is ~0%: scheduler accumulated 0 jiffies of utime+stime over a 6s idle window during a 30s --delay (my #1 requirement, satisfied).
Official tests/crash_recovery.sh passes pass=4 fail=0 including the must-fail no-recover control (R2').
All hostile-input regressions reachable from this challenge are fixed (F7/F1/F4) with clean broker.log and nonzero publisher rc.
The at-most-once-modulo-crash semantics are honestly documented in DEMO 'Why it's honest' and README delivery contract.
comparison_to_expectations: Round-001 scored 7/10. The core claim (survive + re-arm + 0% idle CPU) holds and the regressions are fixed, which is good. But pushing harder this round as a real operator surfaced two genuine new problems on the exact recovery path the challenge targets: the overdue-on-restart fire is a race a reconnecting subscriber usually loses (3/5 lost), and killing the top pid orphans the scheduler and silently drains deferred state. Both are on the boat-power-cut path I care about and neither is quantified/warned in the docs, so I drop one point to 6.
free_text: Black-box evidence: (1) crash3 (true atomic power cut): deferred=1782185553462.* survived t=3/6/9 down, restart drained it immediately, deferred=[] before any sub attached, b.out empty. (2) 5x fast-reconnect trials: trials 2 and 5 got 'msg', trials 1/3/4 got [] — overdue fire races sub registration. (3) crash2: kill -9 of only the top pid left sched(16)/serve(17)/socat(20) reparented to PID 1; orphan sched drained deferred at t=6 with operator's broker dead. (4) idle: sched utime+stime delta=0 jiffies over 6s. (5) regress_checks: --at 99999999999 rc=1 deferred empty; --delay 1.5 rc=1; --at xyz rc=1; --delay -5 rc=1; pub/sub --help rc=0; broker.log clean of unbound/arithmetic/syntax errors. I did NOT read src/.

claim_audit.claim: a deferred message whose deadline elapsed while the broker was down fires immediately on restart into whoever is connected then; a subscriber that reconnects later does not receive it. That's the expected face of the guarantee, not a bug.
claim_audit.evidence: Observed exactly: overdue record drained on first scan; sub that connected ~1s later got nothing.

claim_audit.claim: fires immediately on restart into whoever is connected then
claim_audit.evidence: Technically true but omits that a sub RECONNECTING at restart almost always loses the race (3/5 trials lost). The phrasing implies a connected sub will receive it; in practice the reconnecting sub usually is not registered in time. Loss probability is unquantified.

proof_assessment.notes: Did not re-run the N=5000 chaos gate this round (out of scope for crash-recovery). Ran tests/crash_recovery.sh: pass=4 fail=0 incl must-fail control R2'. R3 reported fired 1 time across one crash window (dup<=1), consistent with at-most-once-modulo-crash.

dismissal_survived.dismissal: just leave a deferred file on disk and rescan on restart
dismissal_survived.notes: The on-disk-authoritative + rescan-on-restart mechanism works (survives + re-arms). The unpreempted gap is what happens to an OVERDUE record at re-arm time relative to a reconnecting subscriber and relative to orphaned schedulers after a partial kill.
